This works because . means “all the other variables, not including location”.
Looking at the Coefficients of Linear Discriminants. The three coefficients are of roughly similar sizes, so its important to talk about all of them. The ones for logwid and loglen are negative and the one for logpet is positive, so a leaf will have a large LD1 score if:
the width is small
the petiole length is large
the length is small
that is to say, a small leaf in overall size with a long stalk. (Note: if the log of something is large or small, the thing itself is also large or small, so we don’t need to talk about logs here. On the other hand, we do need to translate loglen etc. for the reader. Unless they were the ones that recorded the data, they won’t know what loglen etc. mean.)
How many linear discriminants will be important?
Looking at the Proportion of Trace at the bottom of the output. Almost all of it (99.4%) is explained by LD1 and almost none by LD2 (0.6%), so we would not expect LD2 to be important. (What that means is LD2 is expected to do pretty much nothing in distinguishing the groups.)
In order to obtain a dataframe that contains the original data along with discriminant scores, posterior probabilities, and predicted locations:

leafshape_p <- cbind(leafshape, p)

with(leafshape_p, table(location, class))

            class
location     C America Queensland Tasmania
  C America         79         26        0
  Queensland        29         61        2
  Tasmania           0          4        5


Most of the leaves have been classified correctly, but there are still a lot that are wrong. For example, the Central America and Queensland leaves are sometimes confused with each other, and only just over half of the leaves actually from Tasmania (bottom row) were actually classified as Tasmania.
In order to plot the discriminant scores against each other with the points labelled by where each leaf actually came from we have to do the following:

ggplot(leafshape_p, aes(x = x.LD1, y = x.LD2, colour = location)) +
  geom_point()




Does your plot (Question 17) tell a similar story to your misclassification table (Question 16)? Explain briefly:

Somewhat as its supported by the red points (Central America) being mostly on the left, the green ones (Queensland) being mostly in the middle, and the blue ones (Tasmania) being mostly on the right. But there is a lot of overlap, especially between Central America and Queensland (red and green), and some of the Queensland points (green) are far enough right that they look as if they came from Tasmania.
How does your plot of Question 17 support your conclusion of Question 13? Explain briefly.
In Question 13 we said that only LD1 was important, meaning that only LD1 distinguishes the locations in any meaningful way. On the plot of Question 17, the locations are only distinguished left and right (in the
LD1 direction) and not at all up and down (LD2 direction). So it is indeed true that only LD1 is important in distinguishing the locations.
Find the leaf in row 105 (of your dataframe with LD scores and posterior probabilities). Is this leaf classified correctly? Find this leaf on your graph of Question 17, and explain why its posterior probabilities make sense.

leafshape_p %>% slice(105) %>%
  select(contains("LD"))

        x.LD1     x.LD2
105 -3.439251 0.4560472


This is one of the three leftmost points on the graph, and the only one with positive LD2 score, so it is the point towards the top left, in with the ones from Central America.

leafshape_p %>% slice(105) %>%
  select(starts_with("posterior"))

    posterior.C America posterior.Queensland posterior.Tasmania
105           0.9590306           0.04096935       1.645016e-09


96% Central America, 4% Queensland, 0% Tasmania. Since the leaf is in the Central America part of the graph (on the left), it is no surprise that it is overwhelmingly likely to have been from Central America (and actually was).

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R
text: This project combines two statistical analyses completed in R. The first explores whether self-esteem changed over time depending on treatment regime, using repeated measurements collected from the same participants across three months. The second investigates whether rainforest leaves from different regions can be distinguished using multiple measurements of leaf shape. Together, these analyses show how statistical methods can be tailored to different kinds of data, from repeated observations on individuals to multivariate biological measurements, while still producing clear and interpretable conclusions.

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R
text: This project combines two statistical analyses completed in R. The first explores whether self-esteem changed over time depending on treatment regime, using repeated measurements collected from the same participants across three months. The second investigates whether rainforest leaves from different regions can be distinguished using multiple measurements of leaf shape. Together, these analyses show how statistical methods can be tailored to different kinds of data, from repeated observations on individuals to multivariate biological measurements, while still producing clear and interpretable conclusions.

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R
text:
Part 1: Self-Esteem Over Time

Data
The dataset includes 34 participants assigned to one of three treatment regimes: Control, Diet, and Diet plus Exercise. Each participant’s self-esteem was measured at three time points, recorded as se1, se2, and se3. Because these scores were collected from the same individuals over time, the observations are related rather than independent.


Methods
To account for this repeated structure, the analysis used a repeated-measures MANOVA to test whether self-esteem varied by treatment regime, by time, or through a time-by-regime interaction. This approach was more appropriate than a standard ANOVA because it recognizes the correlation between measurements from the same participant. The analysis also checked the sphericity assumption, used corrected results for time-related effects, and included both an interaction plot and a mixed-effects model to support interpretation.


Results
The main finding was a significant interaction between treatment regime and time. This indicates that the treatment groups did not follow the same pattern across the three months. Rather than one regime being consistently better throughout, the effect of treatment depended on when self-esteem was measured. The mixed-effects model supported the same conclusion, strengthening the overall interpretation.

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R
text:
Part 2: Rainforest Leaf Shape

Data
The second dataset contains 206 leaves collected from three regions: Central America, Queensland, and Tasmania. For each leaf, the analysis used three quantitative variables: logged leaf width, logged petiole length, and logged leaf length. These measurements were analyzed together because they jointly describe overall leaf shape.


Methods
A MANOVA was used to test whether leaf shape differed across regions when all three measurements were considered at once. This was more informative than running separate analyses for each variable, since the measurements are related and may vary together. The project then applied linear discriminant analysis to assess how well the leaf measurements separated the regional groups and to identify the main dimension along which those differences appeared.


Results
The MANOVA showed a significant regional effect, indicating that the leaves differed in shape across the three geographic groups. The discriminant analysis further showed that most of this separation was explained by one dominant dimension, suggesting a strong overall pattern in how leaf shape varies by location. In practical terms, the measurements contained enough information to meaningfully distinguish leaves from different regions.

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R
text:
Visualization
The original assignment included interaction plots for the repeated-measures analysis and multivariate visualizations for the leaf-shape classification, helping show how treatment effects changed over time and how regional leaf groups separated across the discriminant dimensions.
View full analysis and code

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R
text:
Key Takeaways
This project highlights the importance of choosing methods that match the structure of the data. Repeated-measures techniques are essential when the same individuals are observed over time, while multivariate methods are more appropriate when several related response variables describe a single outcome. Across both analyses, the project demonstrates both technical modeling skills in R and the ability to explain statistical results in a clear, applied, and accessible way.

title: Repeated Measures, MANOVA, and Discriminant Analysis in R

     <dbl> <chr>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
 1       1 Control     4     3     3    14    13    15
 2       2 Control     4     4     3    13    14    17
 3       3 Control     4     3     1    17    12    16
 4       4 Control     3     2     1    11    11    12
 5       5 Control     5     3     2    16    15    14
 6       6 Control     6     5     4    17    18    18
 7       7 Control     6     5     4    17    16    19
 8       8 Control     5     4     1    13    15    15
 9       9 Control     5     4     1    14    14    15
10      10 Control     3     3     2    14    15    13
# ℹ 24 more rows


Each subject (in a row of the dataframe) produces three measurements, namely the self-esteem scores at the three times. These are the “repeated measures”. Additionally, the repeated measures analysis will be necessary because the three scores on the same subject may well be correlated:

self_esteem %>% select(starts_with("se")) %>% cor()

          se1       se2       se3
se1 1.0000000 0.6216745 0.5685528
se2 0.6216745 1.0000000 0.1976336
se3 0.5685528 0.1976336 1.0000000


In particular, the self-esteem score at the first time point is positively correlated with the scores at time points 2 and 3 for the same subject.
Running a suitable repeated measures analysis requires the following steps:
make response matrix
set up the time-dependence stuff
run your model as an lm first
feed that into Manova with the time-dependence stuff display the summary of this.

self_esteem %>% 
  select(contains("se")) %>%
  as.matrix() -> response

head(response, n = 10)

      se1 se2 se3
 [1,]  14  13  15
 [2,]  13  14  17
 [3,]  17  12  16
 [4,]  11  11  12
 [5,]  16  15  14
 [6,]  17  18  18
 [7,]  17  16  19
 [8,]  13  15  15
 [9,]  14  14  15
[10,]  14  15  13



times <- colnames(response)
times.df <- data.frame(times = factor(times))


self_esteem.1 <- lm(response ~ regime, data = self_esteem)


self_esteem.2 <- Manova(self_esteem.1, idata = times.df, idesign = ~ times)

Take my fitted multivariate linear model, use times.df to describe the repeated time structure, and run a repeated-measures MANOVA with time as the within-subject factor.
manova and Manova are different functions from different packages, even though their names look almost the same. The lowercase manova is the base R function for a standard MANOVA, where we have several response variables and want to test whether groups differ across them. The capitalized Manova, from the car package, is more flexible and is especially useful for repeated-measures MANOVA because it lets us specify the within-subject structure using arguments like idata and idesign. In this assignment, that is exactly why Manova was used: the self-esteem scores se1, se2, and se3are not just three separate outcomes, but the same outcome measured across time, so R needs extra information telling it that those columns represent repeated time points. The manova function does not handle that setup in the same direct way, while Manova does.

We look at sphericity first, from the Mauchly tests near the bottom. We are first assessing the regime by time interaction, so we strictly need the P-value from the second test (though they are both the same). The P- value of 0.014 is smaller than 0.05, so sphericity fails and we cannot use the type II analysis for anything time-related.
The Huynh-Feldt tests at the bottom. The P-value for the interaction is 0.0099; this is also less than 0.05, so the interaction is significant: the effect of treatment regime depends on time (that is, it is not consistent over time).

For an interaction plot, you need a colour and a group , and they are the same column.

self_esteem_long %>%
  group_by(regime, time) %>%
  summarize(mse = mean(self_esteem)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = mse, colour = regime, group = regime)) +
  geom_point() + geom_line()

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'regime'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.





The regimes do not compare consistently over time. For example, the diet plus exercise treatment has highest mean self-esteem at time points 1 and 3, but the lowest at time 2. Furthermore, comparing the patterns over time: the control regime only dips slightly at time 2, but both the real treatments both result in a bigger drop in self-esteem at time 2 and a much bigger increase at time 3. In some way, make the point that it is actually the combination of regime and time that predicts self-esteem.
Below is a mixed model and we show how the conclusions from it are the same as the repeated measures analysis of variance:

install.packages("Matrix")

Installing package into '/usr/local/lib/R/site-library'
(as 'lib' is unspecified)

install.packages("lme4")

Installing package into '/usr/local/lib/R/site-library'
(as 'lib' is unspecified)

library(Matrix)
library(lme4)


self_esteem.3 <- lmer(self_esteem ~ time * regime + (1 | subject),
                      data = self_esteem_long)

This code fits a mixed-effects model and saves it in an object called self_esteem.3. The response variable is self_esteem, so the model is trying to explain differences in self-esteem scores. On the right side of the formula, time * regime means the model includes the effect of time, the effect of regime, and also the interaction between them. That interaction checks whether the pattern of self-esteem over time is different depending on which treatment regime a subject was in. The part (1 | subject) adds a random intercept for each subject, which means the model allows each person to have their own baseline level of self-esteem rather than forcing everyone to start at exactly the same level. This is important because each subject appears multiple times in self_esteem_long, once for each time point, so the observations from the same person are not independent. Finally, data = self_esteem_long tells R to look for all these variables in the long-format dataset called self_esteem_long. So overall, this code fits a mixed model that tests whether self-esteem depends on time, regime, and their combination, while also accounting for repeated measurements on the same subjects.
In words, this says that self esteem score depends on time and regime and their combination, plus a random effect for subject. The correlation between self-esteem scores within a subject is taken care of by allowing each subject to have their own random effect that moves all their scores up (or down) across all times, like a block effect in ANOVA.
The summary of this kind of model is not very illuminating, but drop1 works just fine:

drop1(self_esteem.3, test = "Chisq")

Single term deletions

self_esteem ~ time * regime + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    LRT  Pr(Chi)   
<none>           429.44                   
time:regime    4 437.04 15.598 0.003609 **
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


and this says that once again, the interaction is significant: the self-esteem score depends on the regime and on the time point in combination, as we concluded before.

The P-value of 2.2 × 10−16 is (much) less than 0.05, so the location does have an effect on one or more of the response variables, or some combination of them.
Conclusions from MANOVA are always vague (even vaguer than from the F -test in an ANOVA), also a discriminant analysis follows in an attempt to find out what is different about the leaves from the different locations.
he categorical variable is treated as the response (even though it is actually explanatory) and the quantitative variables are treated as explanatory (even though they are really responses):

leafshape.2 <- lda(location ~ loglen + logpet + logwid, data = leafshape)

This works because . means “all the other variables, not including location”.
Looking at the Coefficients of Linear Discriminants. The three coefficients are of roughly similar sizes, so its important to talk about all of them. The ones for logwid and loglen are negative and the one for logpet is positive, so a leaf will have a large LD1 score if:
the width is small
the petiole length is large
the length is small
that is to say, a small leaf in overall size with a long stalk. (Note: if the log of something is large or small, the thing itself is also large or small, so we don’t need to talk about logs here. On the other hand, we do need to translate loglen etc. for the reader. Unless they were the ones that recorded the data, they won’t know what loglen etc. mean.)
How many linear discriminants will be important?
Looking at the Proportion of Trace at the bottom of the output. Almost all of it (99.4%) is explained by LD1 and almost none by LD2 (0.6%), so we would not expect LD2 to be important. (What that means is LD2 is expected to do pretty much nothing in distinguishing the groups.)
In order to obtain a dataframe that contains the original data along with discriminant scores, posterior probabilities, and predicted locations:

leafshape_p <- cbind(leafshape, p)

with(leafshape_p, table(location, class))

            class
location     C America Queensland Tasmania
  C America         79         26        0
  Queensland        29         61        2
  Tasmania           0          4        5


Most of the leaves have been classified correctly, but there are still a lot that are wrong. For example, the Central America and Queensland leaves are sometimes confused with each other, and only just over half of the leaves actually from Tasmania (bottom row) were actually classified as Tasmania.
In order to plot the discriminant scores against each other with the points labelled by where each leaf actually came from we have to do the following:

ggplot(leafshape_p, aes(x = x.LD1, y = x.LD2, colour = location)) +
  geom_point()




Does your plot (Question 17) tell a similar story to your misclassification table (Question 16)? Explain briefly:

Somewhat as its supported by the red points (Central America) being mostly on the left, the green ones (Queensland) being mostly in the middle, and the blue ones (Tasmania) being mostly on the right. But there is a lot of overlap, especially between Central America and Queensland (red and green), and some of the Queensland points (green) are far enough right that they look as if they came from Tasmania.
How does your plot of Question 17 support your conclusion of Question 13? Explain briefly.
In Question 13 we said that only LD1 was important, meaning that only LD1 distinguishes the locations in any meaningful way. On the plot of Question 17, the locations are only distinguished left and right (in the
LD1 direction) and not at all up and down (LD2 direction). So it is indeed true that only LD1 is important in distinguishing the locations.
Find the leaf in row 105 (of your dataframe with LD scores and posterior probabilities). Is this leaf classified correctly? Find this leaf on your graph of Question 17, and explain why its posterior probabilities make sense.

leafshape_p %>% slice(105) %>%
  select(contains("LD"))

        x.LD1     x.LD2
105 -3.439251 0.4560472


This is one of the three leftmost points on the graph, and the only one with positive LD2 score, so it is the point towards the top left, in with the ones from Central America.

leafshape_p %>% slice(105) %>%
  select(starts_with("posterior"))

    posterior.C America posterior.Queensland posterior.Tasmania
105           0.9590306           0.04096935       1.645016e-09


96% Central America, 4% Queensland, 0% Tasmania. Since the leaf is in the Central America part of the graph (on the left), it is no surprise that it is overwhelmingly likely to have been from Central America (and actually was).

title: Python Learning Platform
text: This project is a Python-based interactive learning platform designed to combine lesson access, quiz testing, and user profile management in a single menu-driven program. It allows users to create accounts, log in securely, open lesson PDFs, and complete quizzes connected to specific lecture levels. The project was built to simulate a simple educational application while also demonstrating how Python can be used to manage files, user input, authentication, and data-based program flow.

title: Python Learning Platform
text: This project is a Python-based interactive learning platform designed to combine lesson access, quiz testing, and user profile management in a single menu-driven program. It allows users to create accounts, log in securely, open lesson PDFs, and complete quizzes connected to specific lecture levels. The project was built to simulate a simple educational application while also demonstrating how Python can be used to manage files, user input, authentication, and data-based program flow.

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Objective
The goal of the project was to create an educational tool that goes beyond a single script by integrating multiple programming concepts into one complete application. Instead of only performing one task, the program manages user accounts, encrypts sensitive data, opens lesson files based on user choice, and checks quiz answers in a structured way.

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Tools Used

csv for reading and writing user and quiz data
os for checking whether files exist and interacting with file paths
subprocess and platform for opening lesson PDFs depending on the operating system
bcrypt for hashing user passwords and quiz answer keys
matplotlib for displaying quiz performance visually

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Program Structure
The program is built around a sequence of functions that handle different parts of the user experience.

Main Menu and Navigation
The program begins with a main menu that lets the user choose between creating a new profile, logging in, or exiting. After login, a second menu gives access to lectures, quizzes, returning to the main menu, or exiting the program.


User Profiles and Login
New profiles are created by entering a username and password. The program checks whether a username already exists before allowing it to be added. Passwords are hashed using bcrypt before being stored in the CSV file, which improves security by preventing raw passwords from being saved directly.
The login function then checks whether the entered password matches the stored hashed password using bcrypt.checkpw().


Lecture Access
The lecture feature asks the user for a lesson number and then opens the corresponding PDF file using the system’s default PDF viewer. This part of the program adjusts its behavior depending on whether the user is on macOS, Windows, or Linux.


Quiz System
The quiz feature reads lesson-specific questions from a CSV file, presents them one by one, and checks the user’s answer against a hashed answer key. This prevents someone from simply opening the CSV file and seeing the answers directly.


Performance Visualization
The project also includes a graphing function using matplotlib, which can display the number of correct and incorrect answers after a quiz. This adds a visual summary of user performance.

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Key Programming Ideas Demonstrated

menu-driven program design
modular function-based structure
file input and output with CSV
password hashing and secure verification
platform-dependent file handling
conditional loops for validation
quiz logic and answer checking
basic data visualization

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Code Explanation
The script starts by importing the modules needed for file handling, operating-system checks, security, and visualization. Constants are then defined for the user and quiz CSV files.
The functions menu_1() and menu_2() manage the user interface flow. The functions new_profile() and login() handle account creation and authentication. The open_pdf() function finds and opens lesson PDFs depending on the user’s operating system. The take_quiz() function filters quiz questions by lesson number, checks answers using hashed values, and counts correct responses. The display_performance_graph() function creates a simple bar chart for quiz results. Finally, the main() function connects everything into one complete program loop.

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
What the Project Shows
This project shows how Python can be used to build a small but realistic educational system that combines security, file handling, interactive menus, and structured logic. It also demonstrates how separate concepts such as loops, conditionals, functions, files, and external libraries can be integrated into a single working application. Demo and Full Code
View Full Code
Watch Demo Video

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Demo and Full Code
View Full Code
Watch Demo Video

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Key Takeaways
This project demonstrates how Python can be used to create a multi-part application rather than only isolated scripts. It brings together user authentication, lesson access, quiz logic, and data visualization in one structured workflow. It also shows an awareness of practical issues such as password protection, file existence checks, and adapting program behavior to different operating systems.

It brings together user authentication, lesson access, quiz logic, and data visualization in one structured workflow. It also shows an awareness of practical issues such as password protection, file existence checks, and adapting program behavior to different operating systems.

It also shows an awareness of practical issues such as password protection, file existence checks, and adapting program behavior to different operating systems.

title: Python Learning Platform
text:
Acknowledgment

Built Through Collaboration



Ἡ ἰσχὺς ἐν τῇ ἑνώσει

Strength lies in unity
• Αἴσωπος •


This project was strengthened through shared effort, collaboration, and mutual support. The saying above captures the Aesopic spirit behind this work: meaningful results are often built not in isolation, but through teamwork and united effort.


Contributor

Nicholas Mucci
Nicholas helped with the Python development of the project by organizing the codebase, refining functions and control flow, debugging errors, and implementing bcrypt password hashing for secure user authentication.
LinkedIn Profile



Contributor

Odna Adiyatumur
Odna helped with the user I/O and front-end design of the project by shaping how information was displayed on screen, improving the visual layout and interactive flow, and helping the Python application produce a more realistic, game-like user experience.
LinkedIn Profile

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text: This project analyzes how average new mortgage loan values changed across Canada, the provinces, and selected census metropolitan areas between 2012 Q3 and 2025 Q4. The analysis combines descriptive statistics, graphical analysis, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, bootstrapping, and regression modelling to examine whether mortgage values followed a common national trend or whether they varied substantially across place. Mortgage lending is used here as an indicator of housing market pressure, borrowing requirements, and regional inequality, making it possible to study both time and geography together.

title: Mortgage loan values fluctuations from 2012 to 2025 in Canada
text:
# Install packages 
install.packages("readxl")

Installing package into '/usr/local/lib/R/site-library'
(as 'lib' is unspecified)

#############################################
# STAA57 Project
# Average Value of New Mortgage Loans
# Data cleaning and preparation
#############################################



# readxl lets us read Excel files
library(readxl)

# tidyverse gives us dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, etc.
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.0     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.0.2     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

# lubridate helps us work with dates
library(lubridate)


#############################################
# 1. Import the Excel file
#############################################

# Read the Excel sheet exactly as it appears
# We use col_names = FALSE because the first few rows are title rows,
# not the actual variable names
mortgage_raw <- read_excel(
  "~/Statistical_Reports_Portfolio/average-value-new-mortgage-loans-ca-prov-cmas-2012-q3-2025-q4-en.xlsx",
  sheet = "New mortgage loan",
  col_names = FALSE
)

New names:

• `` -> `...55`

#############################################
# 2. Extract the real column names
#############################################

# In this spreadsheet, row 4 contains the actual headers
# such as Geography, 2012Q3, 2012Q4, ..., 2025Q4

quarter_names <- mortgage_raw %>%
  
  # Keep only row 4
  slice(4) %>%
  
  # Convert that one-row tibble into a simple vector
  unlist(use.names = FALSE) %>%
  
  # Make sure everything is stored as text
  as.character()

# Replace the default Excel column names (...1, ...2, etc.)
# with the actual names from row 4
colnames(mortgage_raw) <- quarter_names


#############################################
# 3. Keep only the rows that contain data
#############################################

# The first few rows of the spreadsheet contain title text and notes,
# not actual observations
# We keep the rows that contain Canada, provinces, headers,
# and CMAs, then rename Geography to geography for convenience

mortgage_data <- mortgage_raw %>%
  slice(5:51) %>%
  rename(geography = Geography)


#############################################
# 4. Classify each row by geography type
#############################################

# We create a new variable called geo_level
# This tells us whether each row is:
# - Canada
# - a Province
# - a section header
# - a CMA

mortgage_data <- mortgage_data %>%
  mutate(
    geo_level = case_when(
      
      # Canada row
      geography == "Canada" ~ "Canada",
      
      # Province rows
      geography %in% c(
        "Newfoundland",
        "Prince Edward Island",
        "Nova Scotia",
        "New Brunswick",
        "Québec",
        "Ontario",
        "Manitoba",
        "Saskatchewan",
        "Alberta",
        "British Columbia"
      ) ~ "Province",
      
      # These are not actual data rows
      # They are just labels inside the spreadsheet
      geography %in% c("Provinces", "Selected Metropolitan Areas") ~ "header",
      
      # Everything else is treated as a CMA
      TRUE ~ "CMA"
    )
  )


#############################################
# 5. Remove the header rows
#############################################

# We no longer need the rows called "Provinces"
# and "Selected Metropolitan Areas" because they do not contain data

mortgage_data <- mortgage_data %>%
  filter(geo_level != "header")


#############################################
# 6. Convert the dataset from wide to long format
#############################################

# Right now the data are in wide format:
# one row per geography, with many quarter columns
#
# We want it in long format:
# one row per geography-quarter combination
#
# This makes it much easier to summarize, graph, and model the data

mortgage_long <- mortgage_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    
    
    cols = -c(geography, geo_level),
    
    # Name of the new column that stores quarter labels
    names_to = "quarter",
    
    # Name of the new column that stores mortgage values
    values_to = "avg_mortgage_value"
  )


#############################################
# 7. Clean the mortgage value column
#############################################

# The mortgage values came in as text because they contain commas
# Example: "223,074"
# We remove commas, then convert to numeric

mortgage_long <- mortgage_long %>%
  mutate(
    
    # Remove commas
    avg_mortgage_value = gsub(",", "", avg_mortgage_value),
    
    # Convert from character to numeric
    avg_mortgage_value = as.numeric(avg_mortgage_value)
  )


#############################################
# 8. Create separate province and CMA variables
#############################################

# We split geography into two variables:
# - geo_province for Canada and provinces
# - geo_cma for metropolitan areas
#
# This makes later analysis cleaner because province
# and CMA data can be handled separately

mortgage_long <- mortgage_long %>%
  mutate(
    geo_province = case_when(
      
      # Canada goes into geo_province
      geo_level == "Canada" ~ "Canada",
      
      # Provinces go into geo_province
      geo_level == "Province" ~ geography,
      
      # CMAs get missing value here
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    
    geo_cma = case_when(
      
      # CMA names go into geo_cma
      geo_level == "CMA" ~ geography,
      
      # Canada and provinces get missing value here
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )


#############################################
# 9. Create time variables
#############################################

# We also need several time-related variables for tables, graphs, and regression:
# - year
# - quarter number
# - quarter_date
#
# Example:
# quarter = "2012Q3"
# year = 2012
# quarter_num = 3
# quarter_date = 2012-07-01

mortgage_long <- mortgage_long %>%
  mutate(
    
    # Extract first 4 characters from "2012Q3" -> "2012"
    year = as.numeric(str_sub(quarter, 1, 4)),
    
    # Extract the 6th character from "2012Q3" -> "3"
    quarter_num = as.numeric(str_sub(quarter, 6, 6)),
    
    # Convert "2012Q3" into a real quarterly date
    # First make it look like "2012: Q3"
    quarter_date = yq(str_replace(quarter, "Q", ": Q"))
  )


#############################################
# 10. Create a correct numeric time index
#############################################

# We want one numeric index for each quarter:
# 2012Q3 = 1, 2012Q4 = 2, 2013Q1 = 3, etc.
#
# We cannot simply use row_number() inside mortgage_long
# because each quarter appears many times, once for each geography
#
# So first we create a small lookup table
# with one row per distinct quarter

quarter_lookup <- mortgage_long %>%
  
  # Keep only unique quarter combinations
  distinct(quarter, year, quarter_num, quarter_date) %>%
  
  # Sort quarters in chronological order
  arrange(year, quarter_num) %>%
  
  # Assign a running number to each quarter
  mutate(time_index = row_number())

# Join that correct time index back into the full dataset
mortgage_long <- mortgage_long %>%
  left_join(
    quarter_lookup,
    by = c("quarter", "year", "quarter_num", "quarter_date")
  )


#############################################
# 11. Create separate province and CMA datasets
#############################################

# Province dataset:
# keep Canada and provinces only

mortgage_province <- mortgage_long %>%
  filter(geo_level %in% c("Canada", "Province"))

# CMA dataset:
# We decided to keep only the six selected CMAs
# Also for convebience we renamed Ottawa-Gatineau to Ottawa for simpler labels

mortgage_cma <- mortgage_long %>%
  filter(geo_cma %in% c(
    "Toronto",
    "Vancouver",
    "Montréal",
    "Calgary",
    "Ottawa-Gatineau",
    "Halifax"
  )) %>%
  mutate(
    geo_cma = case_when(
      geo_cma == "Ottawa-Gatineau" ~ "Ottawa",
      TRUE ~ geo_cma
    )
  )

############################
# 1. Confidence Interval
############################

# We will compute a 95% confidence interval for the mean average mortgage loan value in Ontario across all quarters.

# Ergo, we select only Ontario observations
ontario_data <- mortgage_province %>%
  filter(geo_province == "Ontario")

# Our first step is to Run a one-sample t confidence interval for the mean
# This will gives us:
# - sample mean
# - 95% confidence interval
# - test of whether the mean equals 0 
ontario_ci <- t.test(ontario_data$avg_mortgage_value, conf.level = 0.95)


ontario_ci

############################
# 2. Hypothesis Test
############################

# We will test whether Ontario and British Columbia have the same mean mortgage loan value across all quarters.

# Null hypothesis: mean Ontario = mean British Columbia
# Alternative hypothesis: mean Ontario != mean British Columbia

# So we keep only Ontario and British Columbia
ontario_british_columbia_data <- mortgage_province %>%
  filter(geo_province %in% c("Ontario", "British Columbia"))

# Our next step is to Run a two-sample t-test. This will compare the mean mortgage value between the two provinces
province_test <- t.test(
  avg_mortgage_value ~ geo_province,
  data = ontario_british_columbia_data,
  conf.level = 0.95
)

############################
# 3. Bootstrapping
############################

# Moving on, we will bootstrap the difference in mean mortgage values between Ontario and British Columbia.

# Set a seed so results are reproducible
set.seed(57)

# Extract the two province vectors
ontario_values <- ontario_british_columbia_data %>%
  filter(geo_province == "Ontario") %>%
  pull(avg_mortgage_value)

bc_values <- ontario_british_columbia_data %>%
  filter(geo_province == "British Columbia") %>%
  pull(avg_mortgage_value)

# Observed difference in means
# Ontario mean minus British Columbia mean
observed_diff <- mean(ontario_values) - mean(bc_values)

# Number of bootstrap samples
B <- 5000

# Create an empty vector to store bootstrap differences
boot_diffs <- numeric(B)

# Bootstrap loop
for (i in 1:B) {
  
  # Resample Ontario values with replacement
  boot_ontario <- sample(ontario_values,
                         size = length(ontario_values),
                         replace = TRUE)
  
  # Resample Québec values with replacement
  boot_bc <- sample(bc_values,
                        size = length(bc_values),
                        replace = TRUE)
  
  # Store the difference in bootstrap means
  boot_diffs[i] <- mean(boot_ontario) - mean(boot_bc)
}

# Bootstrap summary
bootstrap_summary <- tibble(
  observed_difference = observed_diff,
  bootstrap_mean = mean(boot_diffs),
  bootstrap_sd = sd(boot_diffs),
  lower_95_CI = quantile(boot_diffs, 0.025),
  upper_95_CI = quantile(boot_diffs, 0.975)
)

bootstrap_summary

############################
# 4. Regression Analysis
############################

# We will fit a linear regression model using the province data.
# Canada is excluded here so that the province comparison is cleaner.

province_reg_data <- mortgage_province %>%
  filter(geo_province != "Canada") %>%
  mutate(
    geo_province = as.factor(geo_province)
  )

# Model 1:
# Average mortgage value explained by time and province
# Interpretation:
# - time_index coefficient = average change per quarter
# - province coefficients = differences from the reference province
model_1 <- lm(avg_mortgage_value ~ time_index + geo_province,
              data = province_reg_data)

############################
# 5. Cross-Validation (5-fold cross-validation.)
############################

# We will do manual 5-fold cross-validation for model_1.
# This evaluates how well the regression predicts unseen data.

set.seed(57)

# Number of folds
k <- 5


# Each row is randomly assigned to one of 5 folds
province_reg_data <- province_reg_data %>%
  mutate(fold = sample(rep(1:k, length.out = n())))

# Create an empty vector to store RMSE values
rmse_values <- numeric(k)

# Cross-validation loop
for (j in 1:k) {
  
  # Training data = all rows NOT in fold j
  train_data <- province_reg_data %>%
    filter(fold != j)
  
  # Test data = rows in fold j
  test_data <- province_reg_data %>%
    filter(fold == j)
  
  # Fit the regression model on the training set
  cv_model <- lm(avg_mortgage_value ~ time_index + geo_province,
                 data = train_data)
  
  # Predict mortgage values for the test set
  predictions <- predict(cv_model, newdata = test_data)
  
  # Compute RMSE for this fold
  rmse_values[j] <- sqrt(mean((test_data$avg_mortgage_value - predictions)^2))
}

# Summarize cross-validation results
cv_summary <- tibble(
  fold = 1:k,
  RMSE = rmse_values
)

cv_summary

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text: This project analyzes how average new mortgage loan values changed across Canada, the provinces, and selected census metropolitan areas between 2012 Q3 and 2025 Q4. The analysis combines descriptive statistics, graphical analysis, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, bootstrapping, and regression modelling to examine whether mortgage values followed a common national trend or whether they varied substantially across place. Mortgage lending is used here as an indicator of housing market pressure, borrowing requirements, and regional inequality, making it possible to study both time and geography together.

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text:
Data
The cleaned dataset contains 2,430 observations and 10 variables. It includes 54 Canada-level quarterly observations, 540 province-level observations, and 1,836 CMA-level observations. The final dataset covers ten provinces and six selected census metropolitan areas: Halifax, Montréal, Ottawa, Toronto, Calgary, and Vancouver.
The main response variable is avg_mortgage_value, which records the average dollar value of new mortgage loans for a given geography and quarter. Time was represented using year, quarter_num, quarter_date, and time_index, while geography was separated into geo_level, geo_province, and geo_cma to support comparison across scales.
Across the full dataset, mortgage values ranged from $108,877 to $577,403, with an overall mean of about $264,144 and a median of about $246,774. This already suggests substantial variation across both time and place.

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text:
Methods
The project began with data cleaning and restructuring so that each row represented a geography-quarter combination suitable for longitudinal and cross-sectional analysis. Descriptive summary tables were used to compare the full dataset, provinces, and selected CMAs. Line graphs and boxplots were then used to visualize national trends, provincial trajectories, metropolitan differences, and variation within each geography.
The inferential section included a 95% confidence interval for Ontario’s mean mortgage loan value, a Welch two-sample t-test comparing Ontario and British Columbia, and a bootstrap confidence interval for the difference in average mortgage values between groups. The modelling section estimated a linear regression using time and province, followed by an interaction model that allowed provinces to have different slopes over time.

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text:
Results
The results show a strong upward national trend in average mortgage loan values over the study period. Mortgage values rose steadily through much of the series, increased sharply around 2020 to 2022, then declined somewhat before rising again near the end of the period. This indicates that time played an important role in explaining mortgage loan values in Canada.
Provincial patterns were not uniform. British Columbia recorded the highest provincial mean mortgage value at $395,185, followed by Ontario at $346,851, while New Brunswick had the lowest mean at $160,792. At the metropolitan scale, Vancouver and Toronto stood out clearly, with mean mortgage values of $456,449 and $427,126 respectively, well above Calgary, Ottawa, Montréal, and Halifax. These differences show that mortgage borrowing patterns were shaped not only by national market conditions, but also by strong regional and urban differences.
The inferential analysis supported these descriptive findings. The confidence interval for Ontario estimated a mean mortgage value of $346,850.7, with a 95% interval from $325,740.7 to $367,960.8. The Welch two-sample t-test showed a statistically significant difference between Ontario and British Columbia, with British Columbia having the higher average mortgage loan value. The confidence interval for the difference suggested that British Columbia’s mean was likely between about $21,101 and $75,567 higher than Ontario’s.
The regression analysis showed that time and geography explained a large share of the variation in mortgage values. In the first model, time and province together explained 91.6% of the variation in average mortgage loan values. In the interaction model, explanatory power increased further to 96.75%, showing that provinces did not all follow the same growth path over time. Ontario and British Columbia in particular showed stronger upward trajectories than several of the lower-valued provinces.

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text:
Visualization
The visualizations show that mortgage growth was not simply a uniform national pattern. The Canada-level line graph shows a clear long-run increase in mortgage loan values over time. The province and CMA time-series plots reveal strong geographic gaps in both starting levels and rates of change. British Columbia and Ontario remained at the top of the provincial distribution for much of the period, while Vancouver and Toronto dominated among the selected metropolitan areas.
The boxplots reinforce these differences by showing that the highest-value provinces and CMAs also tended to have wider distributions over time. Vancouver and Toronto in particular combined high typical mortgage values with substantial variation, reflecting stronger growth and larger fluctuations across the study period. Together, the graphs show that both time and geography were central to explaining mortgage borrowing patterns in Canada.

 View Project 
 View Full Analysis and Code

title: Canadian Mortgage Loan Value Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways

Average new mortgage loan values increased substantially across Canada between 2012 Q3 and 2025 Q4
Geographic inequality was pronounced, with British Columbia, Ontario, Vancouver, and Toronto consistently occupying the highest positions
Descriptive and inferential methods pointed to the same conclusion that place matters significantly in mortgage market outcomes
Time explained part of the variation in mortgage values, but province-specific growth patterns explained even more when interaction effects were included
This project shows how descriptive analysis, inferential statistics, and regression modelling can be combined to study housing-related financial data across both time and space

Based on what you know or can find out about these vertebrate species, do you prefer the 4-cluster or the 7-cluster solution? Explain briefly.
The seven-cluster solution is preferable because it separates species in a way that makes more biological sense. In the four-cluster solution, humans and monkeys are grouped with rattlesnakes, even though humans and monkeys are much more similar to each other than either is to a rattlesnake. In the seven-cluster solution, rattlesnakes are split off, which creates a more reasonable grouping. It also separates dogfish, bullfrogs, and tuna, which may all be vertebrates and may share an aquatic environment, but are still very different kinds of animals. Overall, the seven-cluster solution is better because it avoids combining species that are clearly distinct in biological terms.

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text: This project applies two clustering methods in R to two different datasets. The first part uses hierarchical clustering with Ward’s method to examine genetic distance patterns among vertebrate species based on amino acid differences in the Cytochrome-C protein. The second part uses K-means clustering to group exoplanets according to transformed values of mass, orbital period, and orbital eccentricity. Together, the project shows how clustering can be used to identify structure in both biological and astronomical data, while also showing how different clustering methods lead to different kinds of interpretation.

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text: This project applies two clustering methods in R to two different datasets. The first part uses hierarchical clustering with Ward’s method to examine genetic distance patterns among vertebrate species based on amino acid differences in the Cytochrome-C protein. The second part uses K-means clustering to group exoplanets according to transformed values of mass, orbital period, and orbital eccentricity. Together, the project shows how clustering can be used to identify structure in both biological and astronomical data, while also showing how different clustering methods lead to different kinds of interpretation.

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text:
Data
The first dataset records pairwise genetic distances among 17 vertebrate species. These distances were calculated by counting the number of amino acid positions at which two species differed, producing a square dissimilarity matrix suitable for hierarchical clustering. The analysis included species such as humans, monkeys, horses, donkeys, rattlesnakes, tuna, bullfrogs, and dogfish.
The second dataset contains 101 exoplanets and includes measurements of mass, orbital period, and orbital eccentricity, along with transformed versions of these variables: log_mass, log_period, and sqrt_eccen. Because clustering is sensitive to scale, the transformed variables were standardized to z-scores before running K-means.

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text:
Methods
For the vertebrate analysis, the genetic distance matrix was converted into a dist object and used as input to hierarchical clustering with Ward’s method. The dendrogram was examined to identify the first species merged into a cluster, the last individual species added to a cluster, and the differences between 4-cluster and 7-cluster solutions. Biological interpretation was then used to decide which cluster solution was more meaningful.
For the exoplanet analysis, the three transformed variables were selected and standardized. K-means clustering was first run with 5 clusters, using a fixed random seed so that the results could be reproduced. A scree plot based on total within-cluster sum of squares for 2 through 20 clusters was then used to identify another reasonable number of clusters. A second K-means model was fit using that chosen number, and cluster membership was compared across the two solutions.

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text:
Results
The hierarchical clustering results showed that horse and donkey were the first two species to be combined into a cluster, which is biologically sensible given their close similarity. Dogfish was the last individual species to be added to a cluster, indicating that it was the most distinct late-joining case in the merge structure. Comparing the dendrogram cuts, the 7-cluster solution was more convincing than the 4-cluster solution because it separated species that did not belong naturally together, such as rattlesnakes from humans and monkeys, and it also split apart aquatic species such as dogfish, bullfrogs, and tuna that differ substantially in biological terms.
In the exoplanet section, K-means with 5 clusters produced groups of different sizes after standardizing the transformed variables. The scree plot suggested that a different number of clusters was also reasonable, and the later solution provided a more detailed grouping of exoplanets. Comparing the two solutions showed that some planets stayed with similar neighbours while others changed groups, illustrating that K-means solutions are not nested in the way hierarchical cluster solutions are.

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text:
Visualization
The project used dendrograms to display the hierarchical clustering of vertebrate species and highlighted alternative cuts at 4 and 7 clusters. For the exoplanet analysis, the main visual tool was a scree plot of total within-cluster sum of squares for 2 through 20 clusters, which helped identify a more suitable number of clusters beyond the initial 5-cluster solution. These visualizations were important because they made it easier to compare merge structure, cluster separation, and the tradeoff between simpler and more detailed clustering solutions.

 View Full Analysis and Code

title: Cluster Analysis of Amino Acid Sequencing and Exoplanets
text:
Key Takeaways

Hierarchical clustering can reveal biologically meaningful relationships when applied to a genetic distance matrix
Interpreting dendrogram cuts should depend on subject-matter reasoning, not only on the visual tree structure
Standardizing transformed variables is essential before running K-means on continuous measurements with different scales
Scree plots help identify plausible cluster counts, but the final choice still requires interpretation
K-means solutions with different numbers of clusters are not nested, so cluster membership can shift when the number of clusters changes
